title: Summer Davos 2026, Day 1: AI's energy problem, trade under pressure and the technologies of tomorrow
gist: Summer Davos 2026 in Dalian surfaced a core paradox: AI adoption is near-universal (97% of organizations) yet transformational P&L impact remains elusive, while the infrastructure bottleneck — energy, data, governance — is widening faster than frontier model development. The Strait of Hormuz disruption has materially darkened the global economic outlook and is accelerating deglobalization and minilateralist trade rewiring. China's role as both energy-transition manufacturer and RMB internationalization engine is emerging as the defining structural story of the mid-2020s.

insights.claim: 97% of organizations are deploying AI yet most report no measurable P&L impact, with the bottleneck identified as enterprise readiness — data infrastructure, systems integration, and change management — not the technology itself.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: Enterprise software vendors, systems integrators, and data-infrastructure plays (data warehousing, governance, MLOps) are better positioned than pure frontier-model companies to capture near-term AI value; the 'picks and shovels' trade is more durable than model providers.
insights.quote: 97% of organisations are deploying AI, yet most aren't seeing it move the needle on profit and loss.
insights.locator: AI is everywhere but why aren't we seeing enough impact?

insights.claim: For every $1 invested in AI, enterprises need to spend $2 on change management, $3 on architecture and governance, and $4 on data readiness — a 1:2:3:4 ratio that implies AI integration spend is structurally 9x the model-licensing spend.
insights.speaker: Roli Agrawal
insights.so_what: The true cost of enterprise AI deployment is massively underestimated by companies using headline licensing costs; this supports a multi-year services and infrastructure spending cycle, benefiting cloud hyperscalers, consulting firms, and data platform companies.
insights.quote: For every dollar organisations invest in AI, she argued they need to spend two on change management, three on architecture and governance, and four on data readiness.
insights.locator: AI is everywhere but why aren't we seeing enough impact?

insights.claim: AI currently remains in a 'tools phase' — coding, content generation, and document writing — and is not yet a transformational phase for the broader labor market, with no discernible employment effects observed.
insights.speaker: Jonas Prising
insights.so_what: Narratives of near-term mass AI-driven unemployment are premature; human capital-intensive businesses and labor markets are not yet at structural inflection, giving governments and companies more runway than feared — but also longer lead times before productivity gains materialize in GDP.
insights.quote: We're in the tools phase of AI. Coding, content generation, writing documents faster. It's not a transformational phase at this point.
insights.locator: AI is everywhere but why aren't we seeing enough impact?

insights.claim: Deploying AI without governance is riskier than delaying deployment for regulatory reasons; the governance deficit — not regulatory overreach — is the real enterprise risk.
insights.speaker: Roli Agrawal
insights.so_what: Companies and investors framing regulation as the primary AI headwind are misidentifying the risk; internal governance failures (bias, liability, data misuse) pose larger near-term reputational and financial risk than external rules.
insights.quote: The brakes in a car weren't put there to slow it down. They were put there so you can drive super fast without the fear of crashing.
insights.locator: AI is everywhere but why aren't we seeing enough impact?

insights.claim: AI infrastructure — data centres, energy supply, and regulation — is catching up much more slowly than frontier model development, creating a dangerous mismatch analogous to cars being invented before roads existed.
insights.speaker: Xue Lan
insights.so_what: Energy infrastructure, power grid buildout, and semiconductor cooling/power solutions are critical investment bottlenecks; companies solving the physical infrastructure layer have a long structural tailwind independent of which AI models win.
insights.quote: The infrastructure — data centres, energy, regulation — is catching up much slower compared to frontier model development. That's where we need to move much faster.
insights.locator: AI is everywhere but why aren't we seeing enough impact?

insights.claim: China is targeting 80% renewable energy in new data centres, with battery storage — specifically sodium-ion — playing a critical role, but sodium-ion production will take 3–5 years to scale sufficiently to support AI data centre demand.
insights.speaker: Robin Zeng
insights.so_what: CATL's sodium-ion roadmap sets a 2028–2030 commercialization window for large-scale AI data centre storage; lithium supply remains the default until then, keeping lithium demand elevated. Sodium-ion battery supply chain (precursor chemicals, cathode materials) is a medium-term investment theme.
insights.locator: No power, no AI

insights.claim: China's fleet of 40+ million EVs, each parked ~23 hours per day, represents a massive distributed energy storage infrastructure that could function as a 'token factory' — decentralized compute and storage for the AI economy.
insights.speaker: Robin Zeng
insights.so_what: Vehicle-to-grid (V2G) technology and distributed AI compute via EV fleets could reshape both energy grid economics and edge-compute infrastructure; companies enabling V2G protocols and bidirectional charging are positioned at an underappreciated intersection of AI and energy transition.
insights.quote: When you buy a car, it's not only that you can save money from electricity compared to gas — you can also create a token factory for you.
insights.locator: No power, no AI

insights.claim: Africa holds 60% of the world's best solar resources but capital is not flowing there due to regulatory capital charges that mismatch actual default risk — African project default rates are among the lowest in the world.
insights.speaker: Samaila Zubairu
insights.so_what: African renewable energy projects are systematically mispriced by global capital markets due to regulatory risk weightings, not fundamental credit risk; re-rating of Africa risk premium — driven by multilateral interventions or new blended finance structures — could unlock significant capital flows and repricing of African energy assets.
insights.quote: If you're investing in OECD countries, there's no capital charge or a lower capital charge. But if you're investing in Africa, there's a much higher capital charge. That is a clear mismatch.
insights.locator: No power, no AI

insights.claim: Disruption around the Strait of Hormuz has become the single largest weight on the global economy in 2026, driving up energy and food prices and unsettling global supply chains, with near-term instability — including more tit-for-tat strikes and threats to reopen/close the strait — remaining the base case.
insights.speaker: Sanam Vakil
insights.so_what: Energy price volatility, food security risk (especially in East Africa via fertilizer shipment disruptions), and supply chain re-routing costs remain elevated; commodity traders, LNG terminal operators, and alternative energy corridor owners (e.g., pipelines bypassing the strait) are structurally advantaged.
insights.quote: We have made progress. The ships will eventually start moving, but an instability should be in your minds over the next few months. I imagine more inflammatory statements, more threats to open and close the Strait of Hormuz, maybe even more tit-for-tat strikes.
insights.locator: What next for the Middle East?

insights.claim: The Strait of Hormuz closure is 'another nail in the coffin of globalization,' compounding COVID-19 and the Ukraine war as sequential shocks that are permanently reshaping global supply chains away from just-in-time interdependence.
insights.speaker: Paul Gruenwald
insights.so_what: Deglobalization is not a cyclical phenomenon but a structural secular trend; investors should position for persistent supply chain regionalization, higher inventory carry costs, and a long-run elevation of traded goods prices — inflationary for developed markets, deflationary for domestic-oriented emerging market producers.
insights.quote: Another nail in the coffin of globalization.
insights.locator: What's next for the global economy

insights.claim: East Africa has been acutely impacted by the Hormuz closure through sudden loss of fertilizer shipments, creating a far more uncertain planting season and threatening agricultural output.
insights.speaker: Rita Babihuga-Nsanze
insights.so_what: East African food security and agricultural commodity supply are at tail risk; agricultural commodity prices (especially fertilizer, grains) face upside pressure from this supply shock, with knock-on effects on inflation and social stability in the region.
insights.locator: What's next for the global economy

insights.claim: The US-Iran conflict has materially set back Iran-Gulf state relations, complicating regional recovery even if the immediate military situation de-escalates.
insights.speaker: Sanam Vakil
insights.so_what: Middle East regional integration and the Gulf's normalization agenda face a multi-year setback; GCC sovereign wealth fund deployment into regional projects and Iran trade normalization scenarios are lower probability, with implications for regional M&A and infrastructure financing.
insights.quote: We do have to understand that this war has set back relations between Iran and the Gulf states in a serious way.
insights.locator: What next for the Middle East?

insights.claim: The Middle East is at the start of a pivotal 10-year shift from being a trade corridor to becoming an industrial and value-added manufacturing region, with wars simultaneously creating opportunities for local companies.
insights.speaker: Khaled Sharbatly
insights.so_what: Middle East industrial capex and domestic manufacturing development — particularly in solar, defense, petrochemicals — represent a multi-decade investment cycle; the crisis accelerates import substitution and industrial policy, benefiting local conglomerates and sector-specific JV partners.
insights.quote: The next 10 years for the Middle East will be pivotal and shifting the Middle East from a trade corridor to an industrial and an added value region.
insights.locator: What next for the Middle East?

insights.claim: Over the next decade the cost of dollar funding — both inside and outside the US — will rise considerably, structurally elevating RMB's relative attractiveness as an alternative funding and reserve currency.
insights.speaker: Diana Choyleva
insights.so_what: Rising dollar funding costs create a secular tailwind for RMB-denominated trade finance and bilateral swap lines; companies and sovereigns with RMB exposure benefit from structural currency diversification demand.
insights.locator: China's renminbi internationalization - what to know

insights.claim: SWIFT data showing yuan falling in rankings is misleading because significant trade has already moved 'off dollar rails' and digital payment flows are difficult to track, meaning RMB's true share of global trade finance is understated.
insights.speaker: Diana Choyleva
insights.so_what: Conventional metrics (SWIFT share) undercount RMB internationalization progress; investors relying on SWIFT data to gauge de-dollarization are working with incomplete information — cross-border digital payment platforms and bilateral settlement networks capture activity invisible to SWIFT.
insights.quote: With so much trade moving off the dollar rails and digital payments difficult to track.
insights.locator: China's renminbi internationalization - what to know

insights.claim: The Chinese economy will become the world's largest economy measured by currency appreciation alone, independent of real growth, as RMB continues to appreciate against the dollar driven by China's trade surplus and macroeconomic stability.
insights.speaker: Zongyuan Zoe Liu
insights.so_what: RMB appreciation is an underappreciated mechanism for China's economic size convergence with the US; investors with unhedged RMB exposure (Chinese equities, bonds denominated in RMB) benefit from both real returns and FX appreciation.
insights.locator: China's renminbi internationalization - what to know

insights.claim: China has renewed a 200-billion-yuan swap line with the Bank of Canada, signaling that middle powers — including G7 members — are actively hedging dollar dependency through bilateral RMB arrangements.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: The Bank of Canada–PBoC swap renewal is a significant signal that even traditionally dollar-aligned G7 members are building RMB liquidity backstops; this normalizes RMB swap lines globally and reduces the stigma of currency diversification away from the dollar.
insights.locator: China's renminbi internationalization - what to know

insights.claim: RMB's safety-asset advantage is enhanced in a world diversifying away from oil, as China's currency is increasingly linked to commodity trading in non-oil sectors where China dominates.
insights.speaker: Zongyuan Zoe Liu
insights.so_what: The petrodollar system's gradual erosion as oil cedes centrality to critical minerals and green commodities creates structural space for RMB in commodity settlement; companies in battery metals, rare earths, and green commodity trading benefit from RMB pricing acceptance.
insights.locator: China's renminbi internationalization - what to know

insights.claim: The WTO-led multilateral trading system is in structural crisis, and minilateralism — smaller strategic clubs of like-minded nations forming regional or bilateral agreements — is becoming the de facto replacement architecture for global trade governance.
insights.speaker: Kristen Hopewell
insights.so_what: Trade policy risk for multinationals is rising as rules fragment across bilateral and regional agreements; companies must manage multiple overlapping regulatory regimes, increasing compliance costs and supply chain complexity — a persistent drag on margins for globally diversified businesses.
insights.quote: These types of agreements are an important alternative given the crisis in the multilateral trading system and they represent an important effort to try to shore up rules-based trade.
insights.locator: Minilateralism and global trade

insights.claim: Canada has signed over 20 economic and security partnerships with countries and blocs, explicitly embracing minilateralism as its primary trade strategy given the failure of traditional multilateral institutions.
insights.speaker: Kody Blois
insights.so_what: Canada is aggressively building trade optionality outside the US-Canada-Mexico framework; Canadian companies and investors benefit from expanded market access, but also face increased regulatory complexity as the country navigates multiple overlapping partnership obligations.
insights.locator: Minilateralism and global trade

insights.claim: The self-sufficiency impulse is now mainstream enough that 'autarky' — a word previously unthinkable at Davos — anchors a session title, signaling a fundamental paradigm shift in how the world's elite views economic openness.
insights.speaker: Yuen Yuen Ang
insights.so_what: Autarkic pressures will drive persistent domestic manufacturing investment (reshoring, friendshoring), industrial policy subsidies, and tariff escalation; investors in domestic-facing industrial, defense, and critical-infrastructure sectors are structurally advantaged over globally exposed consumer and retail businesses.
insights.quote: It is quite a statement of our times that this word has been brought back.
insights.locator: At what price self-sufficiency?

insights.claim: Australia's economic future is 'open-economy economic nationalism' — exporting decarbonized commodities like green iron to a rapidly decarbonizing Asia — rather than either pure autarky or unrestricted free trade.
insights.speaker: Elizabeth Thurbon
insights.so_what: Green commodity processing (green hydrogen, green iron, green ammonia) from resource-rich nations like Australia is a large-scale investment theme; companies enabling the infrastructure to produce, ship, and utilize decarbonized commodities will benefit from both industrial policy support and structural demand from decarbonizing Asia.
insights.quote: This is a kind of open-economy economic nationalism, which is where the future lies.
insights.locator: At what price self-sufficiency?

insights.claim: China's Belt and Road Initiative has entered a 'new era' post-2025, shifting from large-scale hard infrastructure (railways, ports) to smaller, greener projects — renewable energy, digital infrastructure, and technology parks — attracting 150 countries and launching 3,000 cooperation projects with over 1 trillion RMB invested in the first decade.
insights.speaker: Liu Xiaoming
insights.so_what: The BRI's green pivot redirects massive Chinese financing capacity toward renewable energy projects in the Global South; Chinese clean energy equipment manufacturers (solar, batteries, wind), EPC contractors, and state banks (ICBC) are positioned to dominate the buildout of emerging-market energy infrastructure.
insights.locator: How the future is green for China's Belt and Road initiative

insights.claim: Kazakhstan has invested over $35 billion in transport and transit infrastructure for BRI connectivity and serves as the passage for approximately 85% of rail freight between China and Europe, making it the single most critical land corridor in the Eurasian trade architecture.
insights.speaker: Olzhas Bektenov
insights.so_what: Kazakhstan is a structural choke-point and beneficiary of China-Europe overland trade; Kazakh infrastructure, logistics, and free-zone assets have outsized strategic value as maritime routes face geopolitical disruption — investment in Kazakh logistics and transit infrastructure carries strategic diversification premium.
insights.locator: How the future is green for China's Belt and Road initiative

insights.claim: The average new car buyer in China is 35 years old versus 55 years old in the US, making Chinese consumers structurally more receptive to software-defined vehicle features and EV adoption — a key competitive advantage for Chinese automakers.
insights.speaker: Elaine Buckberg
insights.so_what: Chinese automakers have a favorable demand-side demographic tailwind for technology-rich EVs; the Chinese auto market functions as a living lab for software-defined vehicle innovation that Western OEMs cannot easily replicate given their older buyer demographics.
insights.locator: They're much more than cars now

insights.claim: China's ability to transform its auto industry faster than Western incumbents stems from 'all-new companies starting with a clean sheet of paper' — greenfield entrants unburdened by legacy platform, dealer, and supplier relationships.
insights.speaker: Elaine Buckberg
insights.so_what: Legacy Western and Japanese automakers face a structural disadvantage vs. Chinese EV startups that cannot be overcome through incremental investment; the auto supply chain disruption will extend to Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers whose products are tied to ICE platforms.
insights.locator: They're much more than cars now

insights.claim: Software-defined vehicles bring a material cybersecurity risk that scales with proliferation — the dramatic technology revamp of passenger vehicles creates new attack surfaces that did not exist in the ICE era.
insights.speaker: Elaine Buckberg
insights.so_what: Automotive cybersecurity is a structurally growing market as vehicle software complexity increases; automotive-grade security software, OTA update management, and regulatory compliance platforms will see growing demand across all major auto markets.
insights.locator: They're much more than cars now

themes.theme: Gender Gap in AI Building & Venture Capital

insights.claim: Women-led companies receive only approximately 2% of venture capital despite delivering what is claimed to be 2.5 times better outcomes than male-led companies — a significant capital misallocation with measurable performance implications.
insights.speaker: Joanna Riley
insights.so_what: The gender funding gap in venture capital represents either a large systemic inefficiency (alpha opportunity for investors willing to back women founders) or a case where 'outcomes' are measured on metrics that don't fully align with VC return models; regardless, gender-lens investing is increasingly backed by institutional LPs seeking both returns and impact.
insights.locator: Women are using AI. But if they're not building it, biased products follow

insights.claim: AI products built without adequate female representation in the development and product management process will embed biases — particularly in health, skincare, and consumer products — that harm women as the majority-consumer group.
insights.speaker: Joanna Riley
insights.so_what: AI product liability and reputational risk is material for companies deploying AI in health, beauty, and consumer goods without diverse development teams; regulatory scrutiny of algorithmic bias is increasing globally, creating compliance and litigation risk.
insights.quote: If not, we are going to build biased products. Products built on fundamentally male data — if you think about women's skin, women's health — women are not going to have the same outcome.
insights.locator: Women are using AI. But if they're not building it, biased products follow

insights.claim: The narrative that US GDP growth is driven by the AI boom is misleading because almost all equipment loaded into newly built US AI data centres is imported — meaning the capex boom contributes far less to GDP growth than commonly assumed.
insights.speaker: Matthew Klein
insights.so_what: The AI investment boom's contribution to US GDP is overstated because capex is largely import-intensive (NVIDIA chips manufactured in Taiwan, servers assembled in Asia); the actual domestic value-add and multiplier effect is lower, tempering bullish US growth consensus forecasts tied to AI capex.
insights.locator: Which numbers tell the clearest story about the economy?

insights.claim: The term 'overcapacity' as applied to China's green technology production (solar panels, EVs) is analytically misleading and politically motivated, as it ignores that global demand for green technology should be far higher given climate imperatives.
insights.speaker: Matthew Klein
insights.so_what: Framing Chinese green-tech production as 'overcapacity' — rather than accelerated delivery of necessary climate solutions — is more useful for trade-war politics than for investors; the implied global green tech demand is larger than current markets suggest, supporting long-run demand for Chinese solar, battery, and EV exports.
insights.quote: I strongly dislike this word... I get why people use it, but I don't think it's helpful.
insights.locator: Which numbers tell the clearest story about the economy?

insights.claim: A 'little inflation' is a positive signal for China's economy because the country's primary macro risk is deflation reflecting economic sluggishness — the opposite dynamic to inflation risk in Western economies.
insights.speaker: Shen Jianguang
insights.so_what: China's domestic demand recovery signal should be tracked through inflation as an indicator of strength, not weakness; central bank and fiscal policy responses in China are calibrated to avoid deflation, implying continued stimulus bias that is bullish for Chinese domestic consumer and property sectors over the medium term.
insights.quote: A little inflation is actually a positive.
insights.locator: What's next for the global economy

insights.claim: 22% of job roles across all industries will be reshaped — not eliminated — by AI in the coming years, requiring workers to demonstrate AI competency as a baseline, alongside deep domain knowledge in areas like physics for manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Xie Haiqin
insights.so_what: The AI-driven job reshaping thesis is more nuanced than wholesale job displacement; education and retraining in AI-augmentation skills and foundational STEM (physics, engineering) is the more investable theme, benefiting technical education platforms and corporate training companies.
insights.quote: 22% of job roles will be reshaped by AI in the coming years.
insights.locator: How smart factories put people first in the learning process

insights.claim: The biggest EBITDA impact of AI in manufacturing is in operations — particularly changeover optimization between production runs — but this is also the hardest area to deploy AI due to the complexity of core production environments.
insights.speaker: Annie Lu
insights.so_what: Industrial AI vendors focused on operational optimization (production scheduling, changeover, OEE improvement) rather than front-office or generative AI applications have the highest revenue-impact potential and defensible moats within manufacturing enterprises.
insights.quote: One of the biggest EBITDA impacts of AI in manufacturing organizations is actually in operations, but it's also one of the biggest challenges.
insights.locator: How smart factories put people first in the learning process
